entry.title: Re: Good receipt and label printing for mixed purchase or...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question.

To add another aspect, I asked our internal chatbot for support. Here's the response. If the question is still open, please check whether the response resolves the issue.

To address the issue of printing goods receipt labels for mixed purchase orders containing both regular production materials and free text items, you can consider the following approach:

Use of Output Parameter Determination (OPD)

  • Configure Output Types: Ensure that your Output Parameter Determination settings are configured to handle different item categories within a purchase order. This might involve setting up specific rules for label printing based on item types.

Separate Processing

  • Batch Processing: Instead of manually separating items, consider using batch processing or automation scripts that can identify and separate free text items from regular production materials. This can streamline the process and reduce manual effort.

Custom Logic

  • Implement Custom Logic: If the standard configuration does not meet your needs, consider implementing custom logic using Business Add-Ins (BAdIs) to automate the identification and processing of different item types within a purchase order.

Consult SAP Support

  • SAP Support: If these options do not resolve the issue, consulting with SAP support or your IT department for a more tailored solution might be necessary.

Unfortunately, without the ability to upload Excel files directly, you might need to explore alternative methods for sharing configuration files, such as using shared drives or cloud storage services.

For more information, see Output Parameter Determination.

Kind regards, 

Henrike

entry.title: Re: SAP S4/hana public cloud RAP to SAP build automation...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the long delay.

I asked our internal chatbot for support. Here's the response. If the question is still open, can you please check whether the response resolves the issue?

It seems like you are encountering an issue with the workflow not being created in SAP Build Process Automation, and you are receiving a 404 error on the outbound communication. Here are a few steps and considerations that might help resolve the issue:

Check Communication Arrangement

Ensure that the communication arrangement SAP_COM_0863 is correctly set up. Create Business Partners in S/4HANA Cloud with an … - SAP Community This includes verifying that the service URL is correctly configured to point to the SAP Build Process Automation endpoint. The path /public/workflow/rest/v1/workflow-instances should be accessible, and you might need to include the environmentId as a query parameter if required by your setup.

Verify API Endpoint

The 404 error suggests that the endpoint might not be correctly specified. Double-check the API endpoint URL in your communication arrangement and ensure it matches the expected format, including any necessary query parameters like environmentId.

Check Authorization

Ensure that the communication user has the necessary roles and permissions to access the SAP Build Process Automation services. This includes roles like WorkflowInitiator and any other roles required for your specific setup.

Debugging Tips

  • Use tools like Postman to manually test the API endpoint and verify that it is accessible and returns the expected responses.
  • Check the logs in SAP BTP to see if there are any additional error messages or details that can provide more context on the 404 error.

Additional Configuration

If your setup requires specifying the environmentId, ensure that this is included in the API calls. This might need to be configured in the communication arrangement or within your RAP method logic.

By following these steps, you should be able to identify and resolve the issue with the workflow not being created.

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Upload/Export Success Facors Employee Central Profile Photos through Job
entry.content:
Bulk Upload Profile Photos job

Uploading Multiple Profile Photos

Create a job request to upload multiple profile photos for employees to the system in the Scheduled Job Manager admin tool.

Prerequisites

  • You've prepared profile photos or compressed photos, and a CSV mapping file that meet the requirements specified in File Requirements. If you want to schedule multiple jobs that run in parallel, prepare one CSV file for each job.
  • You've uploaded the photos and CSV files to a directory on the SFTP server.

For security reasons, we recommend that you use SAP SuccessFactors hosted SFTP server. In case you don't have an SFTP account, contact your Partner or Technical Support.

  • You have the following permissions:
    • Admin Center Permissions  Monitor Scheduled Jobs
    • Admin Center Permissions  Manage Scheduled Jobs
    • Manage User  Bulk Upload Profile Photos

 

Note

This job can be scheduled to run up to 10 times per day in the Admin Center. The daily execution times are counted based on server time.

 

Procedure

  1. Go to Admin Center  Scheduled Job Manager.
  2. On the Job Scheduler tab, choose Create Job Request.
  3. In the Job Definition section, enter the following information.

Setting

Description

Job Name

The name given to the job.

Job Type

Select the option Bulk Upload Profile Photos.

Job Owner

The job owner must be the person who created this job request.

 

  1. Select an appropriate option for Character Encoding.

Remember

Photo names must follow the character encoding specified for the job.

  1. Optional: Check one or more options in Job Parameters settings if necessary.

Options

Description

Upload Zipped Photos

Select this option if you're uploading photos in ZIP files.

Delete All Photos from the FTP Server After Upload

All photos, including compressed photos, are deleted from the server once they are uploaded.

Note

You must select this option if you use an SAP SuccessFactors hosted SFTP server.

  1. In the FTP Configuration section, enter information about the server access and file access.

Note

We recommend that you use an SFTP server for stability and security.

  1. In the Job Occurrence section, define how frequently you want the job to run.
  2. In the Notification section, define who receives email notifications besides the job owner.
  3. To finish, choose one of two options:
    • Choose Submit to save the job request and submit it to the job scheduler, so that the job is scheduled to run at the specified time.
    • Choose Save to save the job request, but not submit it. Configurations are saved but the job isn't scheduled to run yet.

Next Steps

You can monitor and manage the job request in the Scheduled Job Manager admin tool. See Related Information for details.

After the job is completed, two entries are displayed on the Job Monitor tab in Scheduled Job Manager in the following order:

  1. A job named Triggered by the Job Request <Your Job Request ID> of the job type Batch Upload Employee Photos: this is a job in Provisioning triggered by your job request.
  2. The job request that you created

Go to Actions click on Run it now, you can check Job status in Job Monitor

After completion of job Profile photo updated in Employee profile

Note: For uploading you need to store the photos and .csv file need to upload in SFTP server first, the above case I have created folder as Photo and File path as InstanceidPhotodate

File name as CSV

 

Bulk Export Profile Photos job

Exporting Multiple Profile Photos

Create a job request to export multiple profile photos for employees at once in the Scheduled Job Manager admin tool.

Prerequisites

You have the following permissions:

  • Admin Center Permissions  Monitor Scheduled Jobs
  • Admin Center Permissions  Manage Scheduled Jobs
  • Manage User  Bulk Export Profile Photos

Context

Note

This job can be scheduled to run up to 10 times a day in the Admin Center. The daily execution times are counted based on server time.

Procedure

  1. Go to Admin Center  Scheduled Job Manager.
  2. On the Job Scheduler tab, choose Create Job Request.
  3. Create a job request of the job type Bulk Export Profile Photos.

The job owner must be the person who created this job request.

  1. Fill in all the required fields.

Note

    • Character Encoding: Photo names must follow the character encoding specified for the job.
    • File Name: Because file names of the exported files are generated by the system after exported, you can enter any text in the required field.
    • Encryption: Choose None for this field. Because of large data volume, currently we don't support file encryption for bulk exporting employee photos.
  1. To finish, choose one of two options:
    • Choose Submit to save the job request and submit it to the job scheduler, so that the job is scheduled to run at the specified time.
    • Choose Save to save the job request, but not submit it. Configurations are saved but the job isn't scheduled to run yet.

Results

After the job is completed, two entries are displayed on the Job Monitor tab in Scheduled Job Manager in the following order:

  1. A job named Triggered by the Job Request <Your Job Request ID> of the job type Batch Export Employee Photos: this is a job in Provisioning triggered by your job request.
  2. The job request that you created

One or more ZIP files consisting of a CSV file and photo files are exported. A ZIP file contains a maximum of 5000 photos. In the CSV file, there are "username" and "filename" columns and the "filename" column contains the corresponding photo names. The exported ZIP file names follow this pattern: <instanceID>_photos_<YYMMDD>_<timestamp>.zip.

You receive an email about the export results.

Remember

As a customer, you don't have access to Provisioning. To complete tasks in Provisioning, contact your implementation partner or Account Executive. For any non-implementation tasks, contact Technical Support.

Next Steps

You can monitor and manage the job request in the Scheduled Job Manager admin tool. See Related Information for details.

Tip

You can find more information about the job progress in the run details of the triggered job Triggered by the Job Request <Your Job Request ID>.

Go to Actions click on Run it now, you can check Job status in Job Monitor

After completion of job, Profile photos downloaded in SFTP server in the defined path

Login into SFTP server with URl to check the Exported profile photos

Reference KBS:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/2220377       

https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/managing-user-information/uploading-multiple-profile-photos

https://help.sap.com/docs/successfactors-platform/managing-user-information/exporting-multiple-profile-photos

entry.title: Securing SAP Agentic AI for the Autonomous Enterprise
entry.content:
(Jana Subramanian serves as APJ Principal Cybersecurity and Compliance Advisor. As part of his responsibilities, Jana helps with strategic customer engagements related to topics such as AI Security, Architecture Trust Boundaries & Governance, Data Privacy, Secure Data Flow, Multi-Cloud security integration architecture, contractual assurance, audit and compliance.)

Introduction

The enterprise AI landscape is undergoing a paradigm shift. We are moving beyond simple chatbots and copilots into the era of Agentic AI, where autonomous agents can reason, plan, and execute multi-step business processes across interconnected systems. SAP Business AI is at the forefront of this transformation, with a growing library of Joule Agents, 400+ AI features, and more than 2,400 Joule Skills use cases already reshaping how enterprises operate.

But with autonomy comes risk. When an AI agent can approve purchase orders, resolve financial disputes, or orchestrate supply chain actions, the security stakes are exponentially higher than with traditional software. A compromised agent does not just leak data; it can also take unauthorized actions at machine speed across some of the most critical business systems in the enterprise.

This blog explores the security architecture, governance frameworks, and compliance controls that SAP has built into its Agentic AI platform. We will examine how SAP helps enterprises adopt autonomous AI with confidence, ensuring that innovation never comes at the expense of trust.

The Convergence: From Embedded AI to Autonomous Agents

SAP Business AI delivers intelligence at three levels of maturity.

Embedded AI provides over 300 use cases across the SAP Business Suite — predictive analytics, NLP assistance, code generation, and Joule as a user-initiated copilot — all running natively within SAP applications. These capabilities are available in supported SAP cloud solutions and editions, subject to service scope and release availability.

 Figure 1: Convergence of SAP Business AI Architecture Patterns

Joule Skills represent the next layer: over 2,400 task-specific, rule-based capabilities that execute bounded actions with predictable outcomes. Each skill is scoped to a specific SAP module and can be invoked directly by users through Joule or consumed as tools by Joule Agents. Skills are designed for bounded, task-specific execution with predictable outcomes, making them well suited for structured and repeatable business tasks.

Agentic AI is the most advanced tier, with over 40 Joule Agents that can autonomously reason, plan, and execute multi-step workflows across SAP and non-SAP systems. Agents can leverage interoperability patterns such as MCP-based tool access and agent-to-agent collaboration mechanisms, with human oversight applied for sensitive decisions.

Supporting this evolution is AI Foundation, the operating system for SAP Business AI. It brings together Joule Studio for agent and skill development, Generative AI Hub for multi-model access and orchestration, the SAP Knowledge Graph for business-context grounding, and SAP Foundation Models such as RPT-1 for tabular data and ABAP-1 for code understanding.

Defence in Depth for Agentic AI

The cornerstone of SAP’s security approach to Agentic AI is a defence-in-depth model. Rather than relying on a single security perimeter, SAP applies multiple layers of protection across five concentric domains: Governance and Compliance, Model Inference, Data and Context, Agent Runtime, and Orchestration. Each layer reinforces the others, helping ensure that no single weakness becomes a single point of failure in the overall AI security posture.

 Figure 2: Defence in Depth Architecture for SAP Agentic AI

At the outermost layer, Governance and Compliance establishes the foundation for responsible and trustworthy AI. This includes ISO 42001 AI management system certification, audit logging, AI ethics reviews, responsible AI practices, alignment with regulatory frameworks such as the EU AI Act, internal and external audits, threat modeling, and AI red team exercises.

The Model Inference layer focuses on securing how models are selected, accessed, and governed. It covers controls such as segregation of duties, API security, and model risk management to help ensure that model usage remains controlled, appropriate, and auditable.

The Data and Context layer protects the information that grounds and guides agent behavior. This includes safeguards for the grounding and RAG pipeline, prompt registries, prompt and response guardrails, agent memory, and PII data minimization, helping reduce the risk of sensitive data exposure or inappropriate outputs.

The Agent Runtime layer governs how agents execute tasks in practice. It includes the agent execution engine, Joule Studio skills, SAP Knowledge Graph integration, workflow and state management, and tool invocation controls. This is where business logic, context, and actions come together, making strong runtime controls essential.

At the core, the Orchestration layer coordinates runtime controls such as tool mediation, policy enforcement, scoped access, human-in-the-loop approvals, and orchestration across models and business actions. It covers capabilities such as the MCP Gateway, A2A protocol, AI Core orchestration, scoped tokens, policy enforcement, human-in-the-loop controls, content filtering, role-based access control, and multi-agent management.

Together, these layers create a security architecture designed not only to protect AI systems, but also to make autonomous business processes governable, resilient, and trustworthy at enterprise scale.

Securing the LLM Path: A Layered Secure Inference Pattern

One of the most important security challenges in enterprise AI is ensuring that sensitive data never reaches external large language model (LLM) providers without appropriate protection. SAP addresses this through a rigorous orchestration pipeline, with a layered secure inference pattern that applies privacy, filtering, prompt standardization, and grounding controls before a request is sent to a foundation model.

The process begins with the Raw User Request, which is treated as untrusted input. The second stage applies data masking and anonymization controls where appropriate, while subsequent filtering and policy checks help reduce prompt-manipulation and unsafe-content risks before model invocation. The third stage introduces Content Filtering, including controls such as jailbreak detection and malware scanning. In the fourth stage, Prompt Templating standardizes the request structure before it is passed to the fifth stage, RAG Grounding, where enterprise context is applied using the HANA Vector Engine. Only after these controls are applied is the Sanitized Prompt delivered to the third-party partner model.

 Figure 3: Securing LLM Path

Across this end-to-end flow, data in transit is protected through transport encryption such as TLS, while principal propagation helps preserve user identity and authorization context across connected services.

SAP also supports two distinct inference paths, depending on the nature of the task. Path A uses SAP RPT-1 for structured analytical use cases such as classification, regression, time-series analysis, and risk scoring, where model behaviour is more bounded and predictable than free-form generative models. Path B uses LLM orchestration for probabilistic tasks such as text generation and creative drafting, where stronger guardrails and runtime controls are essential.

 Figure 4: Dual Inference Paths

The practical takeaway is clear: use deterministic models such as RPT-1 for regulated and high-stakes scenarios, and use LLM orchestration for generative use cases that benefit from creativity and language flexibility, supported by the appropriate security controls.

Business Use Case Architecture Patterns

SAP provides four distinct architecture patterns for AI deployment, each aligned to a different risk and governance profile. This graduated model enables enterprises to adopt AI incrementally, applying the right level of control and oversight based on the degree of autonomy involved.

 Figure 5: SAP Business AI Architecture Patterns

Pattern 1: Embedded AI carries the lowest risk profile. In this model, machine learning operates directly within S/4HANA applications for use cases such as invoice matching and demand forecasting, while data remains within the digital core.

Pattern 2: Side-by-Side AI on BTP introduces a moderate risk profile. Inference is performed on SAP AI Core via SAP BTP, allowing organizations to extend AI capabilities while keeping the digital core clean and preserving stronger architectural separation.

Pattern 3: Generative AI via Joule represents a higher risk profile because it introduces LLM-based processing. In this pattern, controls such as content filtering, managed infrastructure, and Knowledge Graph grounding help maintain business context and reduce risk.

Pattern 4: Agentic AI carries the highest risk profile. Here, autonomous agents can perform multi-step reasoning and orchestrate actions across systems, which increases both business value and security exposure. As a result, this pattern demands the strongest governance, oversight, and runtime controls.

The broader takeaway is that AI adoption does not have to be all or nothing. By offering multiple architecture patterns, SAP allows enterprises to scale from embedded intelligence to autonomous agents in a controlled and risk-aware manner.

Security Controls Across Identity, Application, Data, and Network

SAP applies security across four critical layers, each designed to provide independent controls while delivering distinct business value. The Identity Layer uses IAS/IPS federation, principal propagation, and AMS fine-grained policies to ensure that every AI action is tied to an authenticated user identity. The Application Layer enforces runtime guardrails through prompt filtering, content moderation, tool allow-lists, and human-in-the-loop controls at every interaction. The Data Layer protects sensitive information across the full AI pipeline through data masking, RAG grounding, no-training commitments, and DLP controls. The Network Layer provides infrastructure isolation through mTLS, Kubernetes isolation, tenant segmentation, and TLS1.2/TLS1.3 and destination security based connectivity, helping ensure there is no cross-tenant exposure.

Figure 6: Multi-Layer Security Controls for SAP Agentic AI 

Agentic AI Component Flow and Security Controls

SAP’s Agentic AI architecture is built around seven security control domains that span the full path from the business user to the backend application. Together, these domains provide layered authentication, authorization, and runtime guardrails to help ensure that agents operate within defined boundaries at every step.

 Figure 7: SAP Agentic AI — Data flow across seven security control domains

The flow begins when a user request enters through Joule. From there, the request is classified by intent, processed through LLM reasoning in SAP AI Core, and routed for tool selection through MCP. Before any action is taken, the selected tool is validated against the Allowed Tools List. The request is then passed through Integration Suite, which invokes the relevant API and executes the business action in S/4HANA. The return path follows the same architecture in reverse, carrying business data responses, API responses, and filtered output back to the user.

These interactions are protected by seven distinct security control domains. Identity and Session covers SAML2/OIDC-based authentication, role-based access control, session timeout, and context awareness. Agent Governance defines action boundaries, human-in-the-loop approvals, scoped OAuth tokens, and execution audit trails. Model Security focuses on controls such as data masking, content filtering, prompt injection defense, and safeguards to ensure customer data is not used for model training. Protocol Security governs the JSON-RPC lifecycle, schema validation, transport encryption, and session persistence. Tool Authorization enforces allow-list controls, scope restrictions, configuration-time lockdown, and version control. API security includes rate limiting, anomaly detection, MCP Gateway controls, and traffic monitoring. Finally, Application Security covers principal propagation, four-eyes controls, data minimization, and audit logging.

Taken together, these seven domains show that Agentic AI security is not enforced at a single checkpoint. It is embedded across the entire flow, from user intent to model reasoning to business execution.

The Journey of a Trusted Request: Nine Security Checkpoints

Every request in SAP’s Agentic AI architecture passes through nine distinct security checkpoints organized across three zones. Together, these checkpoints provide auditable governance across the full AI interaction lifecycle.

 Figure 8: Journey of a Trusted Request — Security checkpoints across three zones

Zone 1: Inbound Trust begins with the user request entering through Fiori or Joule, followed by identity verification through zero-trust controls such as IAS and XSUAA. The request then passes through an inbound policy gate in the Generative AI Hub, where filtering and masking controls are applied before reasoning begins.

Zone 2: Processing and Reasoning covers retrieval through the HANA Vector Engine using RAG, inference in SAP AI Core with tenant isolation, and outbound validation, including checks designed to reduce the risk of hallucinated or unsafe responses.

Zone 3: Outbound Action governs execution through the MCP Gateway, backend processing in S/4HANA, and end-to-end audit logging for traceability and accountability.

Taken together, these stages establish a three-phase governance model: pre-processing guardrails before reasoning, runtime controls during inference, and post-processing validation before action or output.

Joule Studio: Building Governed AI

Joule Studio, part of SAP Build, enables organizations to create, deploy, and manage custom Joule skills and agents powered by Agentic AI. A key strength of the platform is its separation between deterministic skills and more autonomous agents, allowing enterprises to adopt advanced AI capabilities incrementally and apply governance controls in proportion to the level of autonomy involved.

 Figure 9: Joule Studio overview — Skill Builder and Agent Builder with MCP and multi-agent support

Deterministic skills are designed for rule-based, typically single-step tasks, while autonomous agents support adaptive reasoning and multi-step workflows. This distinction is important because it allows organizations to begin with lower-risk automation patterns and expand toward more capable agentic use cases only when the appropriate controls, approvals, and monitoring are in place.

Managing Risks in Agentic AI: Four Critical Threat Vectors

SAP has identified four critical threat vectors in Agentic AI deployments and has embedded corresponding mitigation controls into the platform design.

 Figure 10: Managing Risks in Agentic AI — Threat vectors and SAP mitigations

Prompt Injection arises when malicious or manipulated input attempts to alter model behavior. This risk is mitigated through orchestration-layer content filtering, prompt controls, and standardized prompt templates.

Hallucination arises when the model generates ungrounded, unsafe, or policy-noncompliant responses. SAP addresses this through RAG-based grounding, anchoring responses in verified enterprise data through the HANA Vector Engine.

Tool misuse is reduced through mediated tool access, scoped permissions, and human approval for high-impact write operations. Privilege escalation risk is reduced through identity-aware access controls, scoped tokens, authorization boundaries, and tenant isolation controls

Compliance Alignment: A Regulation-Ready Approach

SAP’s Agentic AI approach is designed to support alignment with major regulatory and control frameworks. These include the EU AI Act through measures such as risk classification, transparency, and human oversight; GDPR and PDPA through controls such as data masking, processing boundaries, and consent-aware data handling; ISO 42001 through structured AI governance and continuous monitoring; SOC 2 and ISO 27001 through enterprise-grade security controls extended to AI workloads; and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework through a lifecycle-oriented approach spanning govern, map, measure, and manage.

Key Takeaways

  1. Autonomy requires architecture. Security in Agentic AI is not a single feature; it is an end-to-end control framework spanning the full request lifecycle.
  2. Defence in depth is essential. Identity, application, data, and network controls must work together because no single safeguard is sufficient.
  3. Model choice matters. Deterministic models are better suited to regulated and high-stakes tasks, while LLM-based orchestration is more appropriate for generative and creative use cases with stronger guardrails.
  4. Trust must be demonstrable. Audit trails, human-in-the-loop controls, and policy enforcement mechanisms make governance visible, testable, and boardroom-ready.

Securing Agentic AI for Business Value

In the era of Agentic AI, cybersecurity is no longer just a technical requirement. It is a strategic business enabler. As AI agents begin to reason, decide, and act across critical enterprise processes, robust security becomes essential not only for protection, but also for accelerating adoption, building trust, and delivering measurable business value.

Regulatory Readiness

With the EU AI Act, GDPR, PDPA, and emerging national AI regulations, enterprises face growing compliance obligations. SAP’s architecture-led approach, with built-in controls for risk classification, data residency, and auditability, helps organizations address these requirements proactively. This turns compliance from a potential obstacle into an enabler of responsible AI adoption.

Customer Trust

When AI agents interact with sensitive financial data, HR records, and supply chain processes, trust becomes non-negotiable. Customers need confidence that their data is protected and handled appropriately. SAP supports this through measures such as contractual no-training commitments, data masking, and tenant isolation, helping provide the assurance that enterprise relationships require.

Operational Resilience

Agentic AI introduces a new category of operational risk because autonomous actions can propagate quickly across connected systems. A defense-in-depth model spanning identity, application, data, and network layers helps ensure that no single weakness becomes a business-critical incident. Multiple layers of control strengthen resilience and help contain threats before they escalate.

Speed to Value

Security should accelerate innovation, not delay it. With pre-built guardrails in capabilities such as Joule Studio and SAP AI Core, development teams can embed security from the start instead of building protections from scratch. This helps shorten deployment timelines while maintaining enterprise-grade control and protection.

Board-Level Confidence

For AI adoption to scale, executive leadership needs assurance that it is governed, measurable, and controllable. Capabilities such as audit trails, explainability support, and human-in-the-loop controls help CISOs, CIOs, and risk leaders demonstrate that AI systems are being deployed with the right oversight and accountability.

Conclusion

The age of autonomous enterprise AI is no longer theoretical. As organizations move from embedded intelligence to agentic workflows, the central question is no longer whether AI can act, but how it can act safely, responsibly, and within defined business boundaries.

SAP Business AI, powered by Joule and built on SAP BTP, brings together security, governance, and compliance controls across the full lifecycle of Agentic AI. By embedding protection into identity, inference, orchestration, and backend execution, SAP helps enterprises scale AI adoption without losing control, trust, or accountability.

For CISOs, CIOs, and enterprise architects, the message is clear: Agentic AI does not need to be a trade-off between innovation and security. With the right architecture, the right controls, and the right governance model, autonomous AI can become a strategic advantage that accelerates transformation while strengthening enterprise trust.

entry.title: Adding new item to an existing PO failing with I_PUCHASEORDERTP_2 in S/4Hana Cloud
entry.content:
Hello All,

I have a requirement where i have to add new items with cost center to an existing PO. I am using below code. 
NOTE : ls_po_num-purchaseorderbycustomer has PO number that i want to update.
              ls_po_item-accountassignmentnumber has the last acc asst number.
              GL Account is fetched automatically by the system in the backend.

  DATA(lv_acc_no) = ls_po_item-accountassignmentnumber.
    LOOP AT lt_vbap_temp ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_vbap>).
      lv_acc_no = lv_acc_no + 1.
      DATA(lv_cid)  = |{ cv_itmcid }{ lv_acc_no }|.
      DATA(lv_cid1) = |{ cv_itmacc }{ lv_acc_no }|.

      APPEND INITIAL LINE TO lt_purchase_order_item ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_poi>).

      <ls_poi>-%tky-purchaseorder = ls_po_num-purchaseorderbycustomer.
      <ls_poi>-%target = VALUE #( BASE <ls_poi>-%target ( %cid = lv_cid
                                 material                  = <ls_vbap>-material
                                 plant                     = <ls_vbap>-plant
                                 orderquantity             = <ls_vbap>-ordqty
                                 purchaseorderquantityunit = <ls_vbap>-purchaseorderquantityunit
                                 purchaseorderitem         = <ls_vbap>-purchaseorderitem
                                 netpriceamount            = <ls_vbap>-netpriceamount
                                 documentcurrency          = <ls_vbap>-documentcurrency
                                 accountassignmentcategory = <ls_vbap>-accassigncat

                                 %control = VALUE #( material = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                     plant = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                     orderquantity = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                     purchaseorderquantityunit = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                     purchaseorderitem = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                     netpriceamount = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                     documentcurrency = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                     accountassignmentcategory = if_abap_behv=>mk-on )
                                   ) ).

      APPEND INITIAL LINE TO lt_po_acc_assign ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_acc>).
      <ls_acc>-%cid_ref = lv_cid.
      <ls_acc>-%tky-PurchaseOrder = ls_po_num-purchaseorderbycustomer.
      <ls_acc>-%tky-purchaseorderitem = <ls_vbap>-purchaseorderitem.
      <ls_acc>-%target = VALUE #( BASE <ls_acc>-%target ( %cid = lv_cid1
                                                          purchaseorderitem = <ls_vbap>-purchaseorderitem
                                                          accountassignmentnumber = lv_acc_no
                                                          costcenter = <ls_vbap>-costcenter
                                                          wbselementexternalid = <ls_vbap>-wbselement
                                                          "GLAccount = <ls_vbap>-glaccount

                                      %control = VALUE #( purchaseorderitem = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                          accountassignmentnumber = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                          costcenter = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                          glaccount = if_abap_behv=>mk-on
                                                          wbselementexternalid = if_abap_behv=>mk-on )
                                   ) ).


    ENDLOOP.

    MODIFY ENTITIES OF i_purchaseordertp_2
        ENTITY PurchaseOrder
        CREATE BY \_purchaseorderitem FROM lt_purchase_order_item
        ENTITY purchaseorderitem
        CREATE BY \_purordaccountassignment FROM lt_po_acc_assign
        REPORTED FINAL(ls_reported)
        FAILED FINAL(ls_failed)
        MAPPED FINAL(ls_mapped).

    IF ls_failed-purchaseorder IS NOT INITIAL.
      LOOP AT ls_reported-purchaseorder ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<ls_reported_po>).
        RAISE SHORTDUMP TYPE zcx_bc_message_wrapper
          USING MESSAGE.
      ENDLOOP.
    ENDIF.

While using this code i am continuously getting error " Numbering of child object does not match parent object (&1)." in ls_reported, only for PurchaseOrderItem - item 00030.

How can i solve this error.

BR.
Tushar

entry.title: Meet RPT-1 — SAP's Quiet Revolution in Enterprise AI
entry.content:
The Problem Nobody Talks About Enough

Every large enterprise sits on a mountain of structured data be it purchase orders(POs), invoices, payroll tables, supply chain logs. For decades, the promise was simple: feed this data into AI, get smarter decisions. But the reality was brutal.

Each prediction required a custom-trained model, months of data prep, and a team of data scientists — and still only solved one narrow problem. SAP looked at this bottleneck and built something different.

SAP RPT-1 Playground

 

Enter SAP RPT-1 — The "Rapid One"

Announced at SAP TechEd 2025, RPT-1 stands for Relational Pre-Trained Transformer, and SAP officially wants it pronounced "rapid one." It is not a chatbot or a text generator. It is a model built to understand and make predictions from relational data the structured transactional data that businesses actually run on. You show it a few solved example rows, and it immediately understands the patterns and delivers a prediction, and that too without weeks of model training or a single data scientist.

You can read about it more on SAP's website here https://www.sap.com/products/artificial-intelligence/sap-rpt.html

You can also read the announcements made by @philipp_herzig in his announcements made on SAP Community and LinkedIn - https://shorto.in/3IpCg3

You don't have to believe me, try yourself. Here is again an investment from SAP for its customers and the world on the one and only FREE RPT playground - Free RPT Playground

Yes, you heard that right! It is a free playground which is accessible and covers sample scenario's/use cases, sample data, and a table showing predictions. 

I have personally tested from the point of view of Customer Success team. Maybe I will share the use case I am working on in a different blog. Keep a watch on my next blog or my LinkedIn announcement. 

Open Source

An open-source version, sap-rpt-1-oss, is available on Hugging Face and GitHub for research and non-commercial purposes

Real use cases across the enterprise

In Finance, an accountant can assign cost centers to invoices with just a few examples from last quarter — instantly.

In Supply Chain, a logistics manager can predict which shipments are most at risk during a disruption using real-time data.

In Sales, teams can identify the highest-conversion leads from a new campaign.

In HR, churn risk can be predicted from compensation history, performance scores, and tenure — enabling proactive interventions before talent walks out the door.

Instead of building and maintaining hundreds of separate models, businesses get a single, centrally managed foundation model embedded natively inside the SAP ecosystem.

Try It & Explore Further

  • SAP RPT-1 Official Page
  • Open Source on Hugging Face
  • SAP Community Deep Dive

Do not forget to read the terms on usage for the free playground on SAP website as below (below is an excerpt from www.sap.com)

The SAP-RPT playground is a demonstration platform. By using this service, you agree to comply with applicable data protection regulations. By uploading data to the SAP RPT playground, you acknowledge that you have the necessary rights and permissions to use this data. The data will be processed in accordance with our privacy policy and applicable data protection regulations. We do not store your data permanently, and it will be deleted after processing. For more information, please refer to our Terms of Service and Privacy Policy. Any given API token must be treated like a password. Never commit it to version control or share it in public forums.

Thank you

Feel free to share your views, learnings, findings and obviously feedback in the comment section below, which is crucial for all of us as customers, partners and SAP.

You can also reach out to me on LinkedIn. Here is link to my direct profile page https://shorto.in/aqB8qD

entry.title: How to Launch Initial Skills Assessment in SAP SuccessFactors Without Overloading Managers
entry.content:
A practical continuation of Skills Framework 101, focused on how to launch initial skills assessment in SAP SuccessFactors using Growth Portfolio, simple proficiency scales, role-based skills, and selective workflow without creating manager overload.

In many customer conversations, the hard part is not agreeing that skills matter. The hard part starts after that, when the discussion moves from framework design into real rollout decisions.

That is exactly where this article picks up. In my earlier blog, Skills Framework 101, I focused on the foundation and why a minimum viable pilot is the right starting point. In this follow-up, I want to focus on the next practical question:

"How do we launch the initial skills assessment in SAP SuccessFactors without creating manager overload?"

Continuation from the previous blog

In my earlier blog, Skills Framework 101, I focused on the foundation: what a skills framework is, why it matters, and why organizations should avoid trying to build everything at once. The core message was simple. Start with a focused pilot, define a practical minimum viable skills framework, and build from there.

That usually resonates well at the strategy level. But once customers align on the framework itself, the next challenge appears very quickly: how should the initial skills assessment actually work in SAP SuccessFactors?

This article is a continuation of that discussion. If the first blog was about what to build, this one is about how to launch it. More specifically, how to start the first assessment cycle in a way that is practical, scalable, and not too heavy for managers.

This article is a practical continuation of my earlier blog, Skills Framework 101, and focuses on the next implementation challenge: launching the first assessment cycle in a way that works in the real world.

SAP SuccessFactors already provides the key building blocks. You can enable Growth Portfolio, configure proficiency level scales, map skills and other attributes to job roles, allow users to manage attributes in the portfolio, and configure workflow for proficiency level changes where needed.

The issue is usually not system capability. The issue is operating model design.

Where customers usually get stuck

Most teams do not struggle with the value of skills. They struggle with rollout mechanics:

  • Who should rate first?
  • How many skills should be included in phase one?
  • Should managers validate every single rating?
  • Should workflow be enabled immediately?
  • How do we keep the process useful without turning it into admin overhead?

My view: do not start with a full governance model. Start with a controlled pilot model.

That means keeping the pilot intentionally light:

  • a small pilot population
  • a focused set of priority skills
  • a simple proficiency scale
  • employee self-assessment first
  • manager validation only where it adds real value

The practical pilot model I recommend

Simple rollout pattern:
Role Skills → Employee Self-Assessment → Selective Manager Review → Pilot Feedback → Scale

For most first-time Talent Intelligence Hub pilots, this approach works better than trying to prove the full future-state governance model on day one.

How to do this in SAP SuccessFactors

1) Enable Growth Portfolio

Growth Portfolio must be enabled before users can work with skills in the current experience. In practical terms, this is one of the first setup checks the project team should complete before expecting employees to interact with skills in a meaningful way.

Why this matters: this is the employee-facing layer where skills become visible and actionable.

Growth Portfolio is the employee-facing foundation for skills visibility and updates.

2) Configure a simple proficiency level scale

Before asking employees or managers to assess anything, define a rating scale that people can actually use consistently. This is one of the most important setup decisions in the pilot.

For a pilot, keep it simple. A clearly defined proficiency model is more important than a complex one. In this example, the organization is using a five-level scale:

  • Beginner

  • Intermediate

  • Proficient

  • Expert

  • Master

Why this matters: if the descriptions are vague, the ratings will not be reliable. In a pilot, clarity is more valuable than complexity.

A simple and clearly defined scale is critical for usable pilot data.

3) Map skills to the pilot roles

This is where the pilot becomes role-based instead of generic. Skills should be attached to the selected pilot roles so employees see a relevant set of skills rather than a broad uncontrolled list.

Why this matters: employees should assess skills that are relevant to their role, not everything that happens to exist in the library.

My advice here is simple: do not start with 30 or 40 skills per role. Start with a smaller set of meaningful skills that managers and employees can realistically discuss.

Role-based mapping keeps the assessment focused on relevant skills instead of a broad uncontrolled library.

4) Use employee self-assessment as the first baseline

Once the role-based skills are visible, the most practical starting point is to let employees complete the first self-assessment. This gives the project team a usable baseline quickly and helps employees engage with the Growth Portfolio experience from the beginning.

A strong phase-one pattern is straightforward:

  • employees rate themselves first
  • managers review only where needed
  • the project team reviews completion and rating quality before scaling

Why this works: it creates a usable baseline quickly and avoids flooding managers with approvals from day one.

This is where the employee experience becomes real during the initial self-assessment.

5) Add workflow only where it adds value

Workflow can play an important role, but phase one does not need universal approval routing. This is where many customers make the pilot heavier than it needs to be.

A better approach is to be selective:

  • workflow only for critical skills
  • workflow only for high ratings
  • workflow only for selected populations
  • workflow only when employee input materially differs from expectation

Why this matters: this keeps governance where it matters without exhausting managers.

Workflow should be used selectively in phase one, not for every single rating.

6) Check permissions before launch

Before going live, validate that the right permissions are in place for administrators, employees, and managers. Many pilot issues that look like process problems are actually permission issues.

Before launch, validate:

  • admin access for Talent Intelligence Hub setup
  • employee access to Growth Portfolio
  • manager visibility where review is expected
  • pilot population targeting

These checks are simple, but they are often the difference between a smooth pilot and a confusing user experience.

Why this matters: if permissions are not aligned, the pilot will create confusion before it creates value.

7) Decide whether Performance Management belongs in phase one

Some customers will eventually want to connect skills with Performance Management. That can be valuable, but for most first-time pilots it should not be the starting point. Phase one should focus on building a usable skills baseline in Growth Portfolio first.

Why: for phase one, the goal is usually to establish a usable skills baseline, not to design the entire future-state ecosystem in one go.

In later phases, skills data can also connect with Performance Management, but most pilots should first establish a practical skills baseline in Growth Portfolio

A simple pilot design that works

ElementRecommended pilot choice
Pilot groupHR roles only
Skill scope8 to 12 priority skills per role
Assessment modelEmployee self-assessment first
Manager roleValidate only critical skills or exceptions
WorkflowSelective, not universal
Review cycleAssess feedback after round one, then refine

This is enough to test adoption, clarity of proficiency definitions, manager effort, usefulness of the data, and readiness for broader rollout.

What not to do in phase one

Mistake 1: starting with too many skills

A broad initial scope usually creates fatigue, weaker data, and lower completion.

Mistake 2: using vague proficiency definitions

If “working knowledge” means one thing to employees and something else to managers, the pilot will not produce reliable insight.

Mistake 3: routing every rating through manager approval

Just because workflow is available does not mean every skill update should become an approval item in phase one.

Mistake 4: confusing system capability with rollout readiness

The fact that SAP SuccessFactors supports these capabilities does not mean every one of them should be fully activated in the first pilot.

My recommendation

Use role-based skills, configure a simple proficiency scale, let employees complete the first self-assessment in Growth Portfolio, and involve managers only where validation truly matters.

That gives you faster rollout, lower manager burden, better adoption, and a more realistic path to scale.

Final thought

Most skills initiatives do not slow down because SAP SuccessFactors is missing functionality. They slow down because the first assessment model is too ambitious.

Start smaller. Keep the pilot practical. Use workflow selectively. Let the first cycle teach you what should scale next.

That is usually the difference between a skills pilot that looks good in design workshops and one that actually works in the business.

entry.title: Re: Table ARFCRSTATE line 0 out of range: table is empty...
entry.content:
I don't really understand, what Microsoft is talking about. "NCo 3.0.21 is the last supported version for 2016" -- What?!? NCo 3.0.21 is out of support for 4 years now, so how can they say it's the last supported version?!

From SAP's point of view, NCo 3.1 is backward compatible to NCo 3.0, with the only exception that 3.0 still worked with .NET Framework Versions 4.0 and 4.5, while NCo 3.1 requires a minimum .NET Framework Version of 4.6.2. But this cannot be a problem for Microsoft, because according to Microsoft, anything older than .NET Framework 4.8.1 is no longer supported by them... So I don't see a problem, why they can't simply update to the latest NCo version.

But in any case: according to Microsoft's reply, their latest BizTalk version supports NCo 3.1.6, so the action item should be clear: update BizTalk and then you can use the latest NCo as well. (Which is by the way, what I had recommend in the beginning anyway: I know - because of 2-3 problem tickets where I have been involved together with Microsoft- that in older BizTalk versions there were still a few bugs with regards to transactional security and IDoc processing.)

entry.title: Re: 403 Error when calling an on-premise S/4 API from SAP...
entry.content:
Usually, 403 can come in two situations:

  1. You have forgotten to allow-list the URL (/sap/opu/odata/sap/API_PRODUCT_SRV/) in the "Access Control" settings of the Cloud Connector. The Cloud Connector replies with a 403 in this case.
  2. The 403 is coming directly from the S/4 backend system.

In both cases, the HTTP response will come with a body consisting of an HTML page with a detailed error message. What does it say?

If your HTTP client (which I guess is BPA in this case) ignores/drops the HTTP body -- which unfortunately many frameworks do... -- then you can still see it in the Cloud Connector logs: if the SCC denies access, it can be seen in the "Audit Log", and if the backend denies access, you need to activate the "Traffic Trace" in the SCC, and then you can see the S/4 systems error response.

entry.title: Job Approval in SmartRecruiters: Overview, Features, and Configuration Guide
entry.content:
Recruitment processes in modern organizations require multiple levels of validation before a job is published. Approval workflows ensure compliance with company policies, budget approvals, and hiring governance.

The SmartRecruiters platform provides a flexible approval framework that allows organizations to configure approval chains for both job requisitions and job offers, ensuring transparency and control throughout the hiring lifecycle.

This article provides a detailed overview of Job Approval in SmartRecruiters, including configuration steps, workflow design, and best practices.

Overview of Job Approval in SmartRecruiters

SmartRecruiters supports automated approval workflows for:

  1. Job Approval

Job approval workflows ensure that jobs cannot be published until designated approvers approve them. Approvers are automatically notified and can approve the request directly via email or within the system.

  • Key Features of Job Approvals
    • Configurable approval chains
    • Sequential or parallel approvals
    • Approval notifications
    • Delegation of approvals
    • Approval visibility and tracking

Approvers can review the job details and approve or reject the request, and the system records approval status until all approvals are completed.

  • Types of Job Approval Chains

SmartRecruiters provides multiple types of approval chains depending on business needs.

  1. Sequential Approval Chain

Approvals occur in a specific order.

  1. Parallel Approval Chain

All approvers receive the request simultaneously.

  1. Per-Job Approval Chain

Recruiters can select approved manually while creating the job.

  1. Integration Approval Chain

Approvals are managed through external integrations or APIs.

Configuration Steps of Job Approval

Below are the steps to configure job approval workflows.

  1. Navigate to: Settings → Configuration → Job Approvals

  1. Navigate to: Job approvals chains → Click New Chain

  1. Enter the Job approval chain details and select a field from the Approval fields on which approval will trigger.

  1. Enable the Pre-set an approval chain button if you want to define an approval workflow. Otherwise, if no approval chain is specified here, users will be able to choose approvers before sending for approval

  1. Select Approval Type

  1. Select Approvers from the drop-down and click on Add to chain, and click on the save button.

Job Approval Settings

SmartRecruiters provides additional configuration options to enhance the approval process.

  1. Remove Approver Access
    Approvers automatically gain temporary job access during approval.
    This access can be removed after approval to maintain security.
  2. Delegate Approvals
    Users can assign another user to approve jobs during their absence.
  3. Authentication Requirement
    Approvers can be required to log in to approve requests.

Conclusion

Job approvals are critical components of a structured recruitment process. With configurable approval chains, flexible workflows, and automated notifications, SmartRecruiters enables organizations to maintain hiring governance while ensuring a smooth candidate experience.

entry.title: Maintenance Plan - Mass Scheduling by Using Multiple Items
entry.content:
Problem Statement

In the current maintenance planning process, mass scheduling of Maintenance Plans (MP) is determined based on the start of cycle date. Once the start of the cycle is set, it cannot be modified for the purpose of mass scheduling start date determination.

Although individual maintenance plans or call dates can be adjusted using the New Start function in IP10, this adjustment must be performed plan by plan, which becomes inefficient when dealing with multiple related plans.

This limitation creates challenges when rescheduling groups of maintenance activities, particularly for turnaround (TA)-related maintenance tasks, where multiple plans often need to be shifted together.

Proposed Solution

To improve flexibility and simplify rescheduling, multiple maintenance items can be grouped under a single maintenance plan.

By creating multiple maintenance items within one maintenance plan, the entire group of related activities can be manually rescheduled using the “New Start” function in IP10.

This approach enables the following:

  • Grouping of Turnaround (TA)-related maintenance tasks
  • Simplified rescheduling using a single plan
  • Reduced manual effort compared to adjusting multiple individual plans
  • Better synchronization of maintenance activities

With this setup, TA-related maintenance activities can be organized as multiple maintenance items under one maintenance plan, allowing efficient and coordinated rescheduling when required.

• Maintenance plan mass scheduling is done based on the start of cycle date set in the maintenance plan. IP30 Deadline Monitoring or IP30H Mass Schedule Maintenance Plans

• IP30H automatically performs a preselection of due maintenance plans, which significantly reduces runtime.

• It allows users to schedule all maintenance plans that are due within a specific timeframe in one step.

• Optimized for SAP S/4HANA, leveraging server group and parallel process configuration to handle large data sets quickly.

• Parameters: Users can define the timeframe (number of days) and choose a server group with the number of parallel processes for improved speed.

• Log: A scheduling log can be displayed using transaction SLG1. 

Maintenance plan mass scheduling is done based on the start of cycle date set in the maintenance plan. IP30 Deadline Monitoring or IP30H Mass Schedule Maintenance Plans

Multiple maintenance items that are relevant for turnaround can be grouped in one maintenance plan, which will allow manual rescheduling as per the new start date from IP10. This way, the TA-turnaround-related maintenance plans can be grouped and created as multiple maintenance items against one plan.

Key Takeaway

🔹Start of Cycle controls mass scheduling in maintenance plans and is not easily changed once set.
🔹IP10 “New Start” allows rescheduling but is inefficient when handling many individual plans.
🔹Grouping multiple maintenance items under one maintenance plan enables faster and coordinated rescheduling.
🔹This approach significantly simplifies Turnaround (TA) maintenance planning and improves scheduling efficiency in SAP EAM.

#SAP #SAPEAM #SAPPM #EnterpriseAssetManagement #MaintenancePlanning #PreventiveMaintenance

entry.title: Offer Approval in SmartRecruiters: Overview, Features, and Configuration Guide
entry.content:
Recruitment processes in modern organizations require multiple levels of validation before an offer is extended to a candidate. Approval workflows ensure compliance with company policies, budget approvals, and hiring governance.

The SmartRecruiters platform provides a flexible approval framework that allows organizations to configure approval chains for both job requisitions and job offers, ensuring transparency and control throughout the hiring lifecycle.

This article provides a detailed overview of Offer Approval in SmartRecruiters, including configuration steps, workflow design, nd best practices.

Overview of Offer Approval in SmartRecruiters

Offer approvals ensure that offers are reviewed internally before they are sent to candidates.
Offer approval workflows are typically triggered after the candidate reaches the Offer stage in the hiring process.

  • Key Features of Offer Approvals

Key capabilities of SmartRecruiters offer management include:

  • Offer letter templates
  • Offer clause libraries
  • Automated approval routing
  • Real-time approval notifications
  • Digital offer acceptance

Offer approvals can be configured based on job roles, departments, or compensation structures.

  • Types of Offer Approval Chains

SmartRecruiters provides multiple types of approval chains depending on business needs.

  1. Sequential Approval Chain: Approvals occur in a specific order.
  1. Parallel Approval Chain: All approvers receive the request simultaneously.
  1. Per-Job Approval Chain: Recruiters can select approved manually while creating the job.
  1. Integration Approval Chain: Approvals are managed through external integrations or APIs.

Configuration steps of Offer Approval

Below are the steps to configure offer workflows.

  1. Navigate to: Settings → Configuration → Offer Approvals

  1. Navigate to: Offer approvals chains → Click New Chain

  1. Enter the Offer approval chain details and select a field from the approval fields on which approval will trigger.

  1. Enable the " Pre-set an approval chain button if you want to define an approval workflow. Otherwise, if no approval chain is specified here, users will be able to choose approvers before sending for approval

  1. Select Approval Type

  1. Select Approvers from the drop-down, click on Add to chain, and click on the save button.

Offer Approval Settings

SmartRecruiters provides additional configuration options to enhance the approval process.

  1. Approver access to the offer
    Remove approvers access to the offer after the offer has been approved
  2. Delegate Approvals
    Users can assign another user to approve offers during their absence.
  3. Authentication Requirement
    Offer approvals can be accessed without logging into SmartRecruiters.

Conclusion
Offer approvals are critical components of a structured recruitment process. With configurable approval chains, flexible workflows, and automated notifications, SmartRecruiters enables organizations to maintain hiring governance while ensuring a smooth candidate experience.

By implementing well-designed approval workflows, companies can accelerate hiring decisions, maintain compliance, and deliver professional offers to top talent.

entry.title: Dos and Don’ts: Key User Custom Fields in Add-Ons Based on SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Have you ever been in a situation where you developed an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition Scalable Delivery add-on and you wanted to add extension fields extending SAP standard objects to your add-on? Are you wondering how to do that and what to consider?
If your answer is yes, then this blog post is just for you. In this blog, we explain which options you have and what to consider in this situation.

Key User Custom Fields vs Developer Extensibility Custom Fields

When adding extension fields to an SAP standard object in your scalable delivery add-on (from now on, we’ll use add-on as abbreviation for scalable delivery add-on) you can choose to use:

  1. key user custom fields or
  2. developer extensibility custom fields.

The pros and cons for and against the two options are discussed in this blog:
Custom Fields in S/4HANA: Key User versus Developer Extensibility
Please note that beyond the pros and cons listed in this blog, you may choose “developer extensibility only”, for example, to keep the software logistics of your add-on as simple as possible.

In addition, consider that the scope of the SAP standard applications enabled for key user custom fields is much broader than the scope of those enabled for developer extensibility custom fields. If you choose “developer extensibility only”, make sure that you really only need to extend SAP standard applications which are enabled for developer extensibility custom fields.

The scope limitations of developer extensibility custom field-enabling are:

  • developer extensibility custom fields are only supported for RAP-based applications
  • developer extensibility custom fields do not support SAP GUI UIs
  • developer extensibility custom fields do not support SOAP APIs

Please note that when you create key user custom fields for an add-on, one more software component is needed that’s exclusively used for key user extensions. It’s created automatically. This happens when you create a namespace and activate key user extensibility for this namespace using the Maintain Namespaces app (details are explained below in the “preparation steps” section).  Remember to maintain key user custom fields in the Custom Fields app and in the customizing tenant, and not via ABAP Development Tools (hereafter called “ADT”) in the development tenant.

Before you can start creating key user custom fields for your add-on, there are some preparation steps needed. Let’s have a look at these preparation steps in more detail.

Preparation steps
1. First, you need a namespace. In the Landscape Portal, go to the Maintain Namespaces app. Here, you’ll see a list of namespaces you already have, or you can request a new namespace. In the latter case, you need an S-user for your company which has authorization to create namespaces.


Request namespace

In the same app, install the namespace in your development system. This is needed to make sure that all the key user custom              fields you want to include in your add-on utilize this namespace. You need to install the namespace in your test system, too, to              be able to transport your development into your test environment to validate it.

2. Next, log on to your development tenant and go to the Manage Software Components app. Within the app, you can create a new software component for your namespace which contains your developer extensibility objects. We’ll call this software component “developer extensibility software component”.


Create the developer extensibility software component

Afterwards, you need to clone this new software component within the same app. This automatically creates the following objects based on the entered name, which can be used in ADT for your Cloud development:

  1. developer extensibility software component (e.g. /S4PET/CLOUDDEV)
  2. structure package (e.g. /S4PET/CLOUDDEV)

Now, log on to your customizing tenant and go to the  Extensibility Settings app where you can see all available namespaces for key user extensibility. To create custom fields in your namespace, you must activate key user extensibility for the given namespace by selecting the “Activate Key User Extensibility” button. Enter a name for the key user software component that will be created within the namespace.

Activate key user extensibility and assign a user to this namespace

Click “Confirm”. Based on the name you’ve entered, the following objects are created for your key user development and automatically assigned to a software collection:

  1. key user extensibility software component (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER)
  2. key user extensibility development package (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER_EXT)
  3. key user extensibility software component relation (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER)
  4. software collection (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER)

In addition, you must assign your user to a namespace. If you don’t do this, your key user custom fields will still have “YY1_” as a namespace and won’t be included in the add-on.

 

3. To be able to create your own developer extensibility objects (e.g. an ABAP class) for your add-on, you first need to create a new development package in ADT in the development tenant underneath your structure package (this was automatically created during preparation step 2).  

Create developer extensibility development package

To be able to program against your key user custom fields from your developer extensibility objects, you must declare a software component relation from your developer extensibility software component to your key user extensibility software component. Just create a "Software Component Relation" object within the structure package. The object must have the same name as your developer extensibility software component where your objects will be located, and from which you want to access your key user custom fields.


Declare software component relation in your developer extensibility software component

Within this software component relation object, you maintain a dependency on your objects in the key user extensibility software component (see also Using Software Components). If you don’t do this, you will get ATC errors in all your developer extensibility objects which reference key user custom fields.

4. If you plan to use your key user custom field in a developer extensibility behavior definition (BDEF) object to specify field control for this key user custom field, you should additionally also maintain the software component relation for the key user extensibility software component (automatically created during preparation step 2). There, you must maintain access permissions for the developer extensibility software component in ADT in the development tenant.

Walking through an example step by step

Now that you’re done with the preparation work, learn how you can use key user custom fields in your add-on following a simple example:
We want to create an ABAP class which selects all Sales Documents from the SAP-delivered Sales Document CDS view. After that, we want to extend this CDS view with a new custom field and select this field.

1. In ADT (development tenant), a new ABAP class is created within the development package (see preparation step 4). This ABAP class contains a simple select on CDS view I_SalesDocument and prints out the sales document ID in the console.

Example developer extensibility class

2. Log on to your customizing tenant and open the Custom Fields app. In our example, we create a new custom field of type Checkbox for the Sales Document business context in the namespace (see preparation step 3).

Create key user custom field

As soon as the business context Sales Document gets extended by a new custom field, the CDS view I_SalesDocument gets extended by this field, too (this has been configured by SAP in that way).



Enable the custom field for CDS view I_SalesDocument 

3. In ADT (development tenant), you can now consume the key user custom field that you’ve created within the ABAP class you created earlier.


Consume the key user custom field in your developer extensibility class

4. Without a dependency declaration in the developer extensibility software relation component (as described in preparation step 4) you will either receive errors by the syntax check on your ABAP class, or by running the ATC check directly. The error message could then look like this: “CDS Entity <name of CDS extend> of software component <name of key user software component> is used without declared dependency.”


Error messages when dependency declaration in the developer extensibility software relation component is missing

To fix the errors, you need to go to the developer extensibility software component relation that you created earlier (see preparation step 4) and add a dependency to the key user software component where the custom field resides.


Dependency declaration in the developer extensibility software relation component

5. Now that you’re done with development, the next step is to release everything and test it in the test system afterwards. First, transport the custom field. To do that, log on to the customizing tenant and open the Export Software Collection app. There, search for the key user software collection created in preparation step 3 (e.g. /S4PET/KEYUSER). The key user software component relation that was created automatically and the development package are already part of the software collection. Add the key user custom field via the “Add Items” button. After that, export it into the test system. When the export is finished, go back to ADT (development tenant) and release the transport request with the developer extensibility objects (containing e.g. the developer extensibility software component relation, ABAP class).

6. The next step is to import into the test system. First, log on to the test tenant and navigate to the Import Collection app. In the list of software collections, find your key user software collection (Type: “Key User Extensibility”) and the developer software collection (Type: “Developer Extensibility”).
First, you need to import version 0 of the key user software collection (containing only the software component) by clicking “Check and Import”. Next, version 0 of the developer software collection needs to be imported. When this is done, you can continue to import the other versions (containing the objects you’ve created) of the key user software collection and the developer software collection. When the imports are finished, test your development in the test system.

7. When everything is tested successfully, the next step is to register and build (Deliver a Product) the add-on product in the landscape portal. The commit IDs that you need can be found in the “Import Collection” app in the details for the imported software collection under “Execution Messages”. For the developer extensibility software component, the commit IDs can also be found in the “Manage Software Components” app.

Now that you know how to use key user custom fields in an add-on, let’s go ahead and have a look at the things you should avoid doing.

Don’ts

Within the key user extensibility scope, only key user custom fields can be delivered as part of an add-on. Other key user extension types are not supported. As a consequence, you can't maintain features in the “Custom Fields” app that depend on other key user extension types such as custom business objects or custom business logic.

One of the issues that may occur is that you may accidentally define the software component relation incorrect or not at all. If this is the case, you’ll get ATC errors when you build your add-on product. This needs to be fixed as described above.

Another prominent issue is that the respective key user custom fields may not have been assigned to the key user software collection before exporting, or that the transport request with the developer extensibility objects that consume the custom fields has been released and imported first. This results in syntax errors in the target system. In this case, export and import the key user software collection with the respective custom fields. Then, the syntax errors will disappear.

Please note that all objects need to be active. It’s not possible to export a software collection which contains an unpublished key user custom field. Additionally, it’s not possible to release a transport request in ADT which contains inactive objects.

Do’s

Another problem that can occur is that SAP may have adjusted the content enabled for key user custom fields, and because of that, you need to republish your affected key user custom fields. Scalable delivery add-ons can be used within SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition only, and all involved (partner and customer) systems will be upgraded with each SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition release.

In general, you need to build a new version of your add-on product once for every SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition release so that your add-on is always installable for customers.

Technical changes by SAP on extended SAP objects (e.g. a migration from V1 views to V2 views) may require that custom fields in the add-on product need to be adapted. In this case, you will automatically get a so-called rework task for the impacted fields within the Custom Fields app to republish the fields. After republishing, you have to export the software collection and build a new add-on version containing the respective changes.

Rework task for the impacted fields

That’s it! Congratulations!
You successfully made your way through the Do’s and Don’ts of Key User Custom Fields @ SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition scalable add-ons.

Please note that this blog has been elaborated by a team of experts. I want to thank Yasmina van Rooij, Christian Fuhlbruegge, René Dehn, Kai Dehmann, and Mario Dietrich Collada for their extremely helpful contributions.

Feedback, Comments, or Questions:

You’re cordially invited to provide feedback, comments, or questions in the comments section below. Please follow our profiles to get updates when we publish more posts on these topics.
Feel invited to post and answer questions here (https://answers.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26) and read other posts on the topic (https://blogs.sap.com/tags/270c4f37-c335-46e1-bfad-a256637d5e26/).

Other related blog posts and SAP help documents:

  1. Developing an Extension Using Key User Extensibility
  2. Developing ABAP Extensions Using Scalable Delivery
  3. Software Component Relations
  4. Extensibility - General Availability of Scalable Solution for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  5. Developing an Extension Using Key User Extensibility
